column4: Ensure Block Volumes are encrypted with Customer  Managed Keys (CMK).
column5: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Block Volume service lets you dynamically provision and manage block storage volumes. By default, the Oracle service manages the keys that encrypt block volumes. Block Volumes can also be encrypted using a customer managed key. Terminated Block Volumes cannot be recovered and any data on a terminated volume is permanently lost. However, Block Volumes can exist in a terminated state within the OCI Portal and CLI for some time after deleting. As such, any Block Volumes in this state should not be considered when assessing this policy.
column6: Encryption of block volumes provides an additional level of security for your data. Management of encryption keys is critical to protecting and accessing protected data. Customers should identify block volumes encrypted with Oracle service managed keys in order to determine if they want to manage the keys for certain volumes and then apply their own key lifecycle management to the selected block volumes.
column7: From Console: 1. Login to the OCI Console. 2. Click the search bar at the top of the screen. 3. Type 'Advanced Resource Query' and press return. 4. Click Advanced resource query. 5. Enter the following query in the query box: query volume resources 6. For each block volume returned, click the link under Display name. 7. Ensure the value for Encryption Key is not Oracle-managed key. 8. Repeat for other subscribed regions. From CLI: 1. Execute the following command: for region in $(oci iam region-subscription list --all| jq -r '.data[] | ."region-name"') do echo "Enumerating region: $region" for compid in `oci iam compartment list --compartment-id-in-subtree TRUE 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.data[] | .id'` do echo "Enumerating compartment: $compid" for bvid in `oci bv volume list --compartment-id $compid --region $region 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.data[] | select(."kms-key-id" == null).id'` do output=`oci bv volume get --volume-id $bvid --region $region -- query=data.{"name:\"display-name\","id:id""} --output table 2>/dev/null` if [ ! -z "$output" ]; then echo $output; fi done done done 2. Ensure the query returns no results.
column8: From Console: 1. Follow the audit procedure above. 2. For each block volume returned, click the link under Display name. 3. Select a Master Encryption Key Compartment and Master Encryption key. 6. Click Assign. From CLI: 1. Follow the audit procedure. 2. For each boot volume identified, get the OCID. 3. column4: Ensure boot volumes are encrypted with Customer  Managed Key (CMK).
column5: When you launch a virtual machine (VM) or bare metal instance based on a platform image or custom image, a new boot volume for the instance is created in the same compartment. That boot volume is associated with that instance until you terminate the instance. By default, the Oracle service manages the keys that encrypt this boot volume. Boot Volumes can also be encrypted using a customer managed key.
column6: Encryption of boot volumes provides an additional level of security for your data. Management of encryption keys is critical to protecting and accessing protected data. Customers should identify boot volumes encrypted with Oracle service managed keys in order to determine if they want to manage the keys for certain boot volumes and then apply their own key lifecycle management to the selected boot volumes.

For each boot volume returned click on the link under Display name 7. Ensure Encryption Key does not say Oracle managed key 8. Repeat for other subscribed regions From CLI: 1. Execute the following command: for region in `oci iam region list | jq -r '.data[] | .name'`; do for bvid in `oci search resource structured-search --region $region -- query-text "query bootvolume resources" 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.data.items[] | .identifier'` do output=`oci bv boot-volume get --boot-volume-id $bvid 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.data | select(."kms-key-id" == null).id'` if [ ! -z "$output" ]; then echo $output; fi done done 2. Ensure query returns no results.

column4: Ensure File Storage Systems are encrypted with Customer  Managed Keys (CMK)
column5: Oracle Cloud Infrastructure File Storage service (FSS) provides a durable, scalable, secure, enterprise-grade network file system. By default, the Oracle service manages the keys that encrypt FSS file systems. FSS file systems can also be encrypted using a customer managed key.
column6: Encryption of FSS systems provides an additional level of security for your data. Management of encryption keys is critical to protecting and accessing protected data. column4: Ensure no resources are created in the root compartment
column5: When you create a cloud resource such as an instance, block volume, or cloud network, you must specify to which compartment you want the resource to belong. Placing resources in the root compartment makes it difficult to organize and isolate those resources.
column6: Placing resources into a compartment will allow you to organize and have more granular access controls to your cloud resources.

Ensure query return no results.
column8: From Console: 1. Follow audit procedure above. 2. For each item in the returned results, click the item name. 3. Then select Move Resource or More Actions then Move Resource. 4. For each bucket item execute the below command: oci os bucket update --bucket-name --compartment-id 3. For other resources use the change-compartment command for the resource type: oci change-compartment -- --compartment-id i. column4: Ensure OKE service level admins are created to manage  OKE
column5: OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure IAM for authentication and authorization, for all interfaces (the Console, SDK or CLI, and REST API). To create, update, and delete clusters and node pools, users must be in groups and those group must have the necessary access. Certain cluster operations created by Container Engine for Kubernetes may require additional permissions granted via a Kubernetes RBAC role or cluster role. One example is the installing the metrics server which requires additional K8s permissions. column4: Ensure access to OCI Audit service Log for OKE
column5: The audit logs are part of the OKE managed Kubernetes control plane logs managed by OKE. OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit Service. All operations performed by the Kubernetes API server are visible as log events on the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service.
column6: Logging is a crucial detective control for all systems to detect potential unauthorized access.
column7: 1.1.1 Using Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Console To monitor and manage operations performed by Container Engine for Kubernetes on a particular cluster: 1. In the Console, open the navigation menu. Under Solutions and Platform, go to Developer Services and click Kubernetes Clusters. 2. Choose a Compartment you have permission to work in. 3. On the Cluster List page, click the cluster's name for which you want to monitor and manage operations. 4. The Cluster page shows information about the cluster. 5. Display the Work Requests tab, showing the recent operations performed on the cluster. To view operations performed by Container Engine for Kubernetes and the Kubernetes API server as log events in the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service: 1. In the Console, open the navigation menu. Under Governance and Administration, go to Governance and click Audit. 2. Choose a Compartment you have permission to work in. 3. Search and filter to show the operations you're interested in: • To view operations performed by Container Engine for Kubernetes, enter ClustersAPI in the Keywords field and click Search. • To view operations performed by the Kubernetes API server, enter OKE API Server Admin Access in the Keywords field and click Search.

column10: The Control plane audit logs are managed by OKE. OKE Control plane logs are written to the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit Service. column14: By default, Kubernetes API server logs and Container Engine for Kubernetes audit events are sent to the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service. By default, the Audit Log retention period is 90 days.

column4: Ensure that the oke_kubelet_conf.json file permissions are  set to 644 or more restrictive
column5: If kubelet is running, and if it is using a file-based oke_kubelet_conf.json file, ensure that the proxy oke_kubelet_conf.json file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
column6: The kubelet oke_kubelet_conf.json file controls various parameters of the kubelet service in the worker node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. It is possible to run kubelet with the kubeconfig parameters configured as a Kubernetes ConfigMap instead of a file. In this case, there is no proxy oke_kubelet_conf.json file.
column7: Method 1 SSH to the worker nodes To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate oke_kubelet_conf.json file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json which is the location of the oke_kubelet_conf.json file. Run this command to obtain the oke_kubelet_conf.json file permissions: Internal Only - General stat -c %a /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json The output of the above command gives you the oke_kubelet_conf.json file's permissions. Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive. Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file permissions on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the permission level of the file: ls -l /host/var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json Verify that if a file is specified and it exists, the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
column8: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the each worker node. For example, Internal Only - General chmod 644

column21: 1. **Identify OKE Worker Nodes:** - Log in to the Oracle Cloud Console. - Navigate to the "Networking" section and select "Clusters" to find your OKE cluster. - Identify the compute instances (worker nodes) associated with the cluster. 2. **Confirm kubelet is Running:** - SSH into each worker node using appropriate credentials. - Execute the command: `sudo systemctl status kubelet` - Ensure the output indicates `Active: active (running)`. 3. **Locate the oke_kubelet_conf.json File:** - Run the command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - Look for the output that includes `--kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json`, which indicates the location of the configuration file. 4. **Check File Permissions:** - Run the command: `ls -l /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json` - Verify that the permissions are set to `-rw-r--r--` (644) or more restrictive. 5. **Remediate if Necessary:** - If the permissions are not compliant, execute the command: `chmod 644 /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json` on each worker node to correct the permissions.

column7: Method 1 SSH to the worker nodes To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run this command to obtain the oke_kubelet_conf.json file ownership: Internal Only - General stat -c %U:%G /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json The output of the above command gives you the oke_kubelet_conf.json file's ownership. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file ownership on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the ownership of the file: ls -l /host/var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json The output of the above command gives you the oke_kubelet_conf.json file's ownership. column19: The compliance control requires checking the ownership of a specific file on worker nodes, which necessitates SSH access and manual verification of the file's properties. While there is potential for automation using OCI capabilities, the specific nature of this control—verifying file ownership directly on the node—requires manual inspection as it involves accessing the underlying operating system of the worker nodes.
column21: 1. **Establish SSH Access to Worker Nodes** - Gain SSH access to each Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) worker node where the kubelet service is expected to be running. - Use the known or provisioned SSH key and the public IP address (or via bastion if behind private IP). - Example: `ssh opc@<worker_node_ip_address>` 2. **Check Kubelet Service Status** - On each worker node, confirm the kubelet service is running. - Command: `sudo systemctl status kubelet` - **What to Verify:** Status line should indicate "Active: active (running)". 3. **Locate the `oke_kubelet_conf.json` File** - Determine the exact file location as it may differ per node/configuration. - Command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - **What to Verify:** Look for the process argument: `--kubeconfig /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json` or similar. 4. **Check File Ownership** - Use the `stat` command on the located file to check owner and group. - Command: `stat -c %U:%G /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json` - **What to Verify:** The output should be `root:root`. 5. **Confirm Compliance** - If the file is owned by `root:root`, the control is compliant. If not, run the command to remediate: `sudo chown root:root /var/lib/kubelet/oke_kubelet_conf.json`

column21: 1. **SSH into the relevant worker node where the Kubelet service is running.** - Command: `ssh <username>@<worker-node-ip>` 2. **Check the status of the Kubelet service to ensure it is running.** - Command: `sudo systemctl status kubelet` - Verify that the output shows `Active: active (running)`. 3. **Find the location of the Kubelet configuration file.** - Command: `ps -ef | grep kubelet` - Look for output showing `--config` and the path to the configuration file (e.g., `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf`). 4. **Check the permissions of the Kubelet configuration file.** - Command: `stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf` - Confirm that the file permissions are `644` or more restrictive (e.g., `640`, `600`). 5. **If the configuration file does not have the required permissions, change its permissions:** - Command: `sudo chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf` 6. **Validate the changes:** - Repeat step 4 to ensure the permissions are now `644` or more restrictive. 7. **Document your findings and any actions taken for remediation.** - Note which nodes/config files were out of compliance and what was corrected.

column4: Ensure that the kubelet configuration file ownership is set to  root:root
column5: Ensure that if the kubelet refers to a configuration file with the --config argument, that file is owned by root:root.
column6: The kubelet reads various parameters, including security settings, from a config file specified by the --config argument. If this file is specified you should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
column7: Method 1 First, SSH to the relevant worker node: To check to see if the Kubelet Service is running: sudo systemctl status kubelet The output should return Active: active (running) since.. Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate Kubelet config file: ps -ef | grep kubelet The output of the above command should return something similar to --config /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf which is the location of the Kubelet config file. Run the following command: stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf The output of the above command is the Kubelet config file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root Method 2 Create and Run a Privileged Pod. You will need to run a pod that is privileged enough to access the host's file system. This can be achieved by deploying a pod that uses the hostPath volume to mount the node's file system into the pod. Here's an example of a simple pod definition that mounts the root of the host to /host within the pod: Internal Only - General apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: file-check spec: volumes: - name: host-root hostPath: path: / type: Directory containers: - name: nsenter image: busybox command: ["sleep", "3600"] volumeMounts: - name: host-root mountPath: /host securityContext: privileged: true Save this to a file (e.g., file-check-pod.yaml) and create the pod: kubectl apply -f file-check-pod.yaml Once the pod is running, you can exec into it to check file ownership on the node: kubectl exec -it file-check -- sh Now you are in a shell inside the pod, but you can access the node's file system through the /host directory and check the ownership of the file: ls -l /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf The output of the above command gives you the azure.json file's ownership. Verify that the ownership is set to root:root.

column21: 1. **SSH into the relevant worker node running the Kubernetes Kubelet service:** ```sh ssh <username>@<worker_node_ip> ``` 2. **Verify the Kubelet service is active:** ```sh sudo systemctl status kubelet ``` - **What to verify:** Ensure the output contains `Active: active (running)`. 3. **Identify the Kubelet config file location:** ```sh ps -ef | grep kubelet ``` - **What to verify:** Examine the Kubelet process startup arguments and note the path passed to the `--config` parameter (e.g., `/etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf`). 4. **Check the ownership of the Kubelet configuration file:** ```sh stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf ``` - (Change the path to the actual config file location if different.) - **Expected secure configuration:** The output must be `root:root`. 5. **If the ownership is incorrect, set it to root:root:** ```sh sudo chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf ``` 6. **Re-run the ownership check to confirm compliance:** ```sh stat -c %U:%G /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf ``` - Verify output is now `root:root`.

column4: Ensure that the --anonymous-auth argument is set to false
column5: Disable anonymous requests to the Kubelet server.
column6: When enabled, requests that are not rejected by other configured authentication methods are treated as anonymous requests. These requests are then served by the Kubelet server. You should rely on authentication to authorize access and disallow anonymous requests.
column7: Audit Method 1: If using a Kubelet configuration file, check that there is an entry for authentication: anonymous: enabled set to false. First, SSH to the relevant node: Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate Kubelet config file: find / -name kubelet.service The output of the above command should return the file and location /etc/systemd/system/kublet.service which is the location of the Kubelet service config file. Open the Kubelet service config file: sudo more etc/systemd/system/kublet.service Verify that the --anonymous-auth=false. Audit Method 2: If using the api configz endpoint consider searching for the status of authentication... "anonymous":{"enabled":false} by extracting the live configuration from the nodes running kubelet. Set the local proxy port and the following variables and provide proxy port number and node name; HOSTNAME_PORT="localhost-and-port-number" NODE_NAME="The-Name-Of-Node-To-Extract-Configuration" from the output of "kubectl get nodes" Internal Only - General kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 (example host and port number) export NODE_NAME=10.0.10.4 (example node name from "kubectl get nodes") curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz"
column8: Remediation Method 1: If modifying the Kubelet service config file, edit the kubelet.service file /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service and set the below parameter --anonymous-auth=false Remediation Method 2: If using the api configz endpoint consider searching for the status of "authentication.*anonymous":{"enabled":false}" by extracting the live configuration from the nodes running kubelet. **See detailed step-by-step configmap procedures in Reconfigure a Node's Kubelet in a Live Cluster, and then rerun the curl statement from audit process to check for kubelet configuration changes kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 (example host and port number) export NODE_NAME=10.0.10.4 (example node name from "kubectl get nodes") curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" For all remediations: Based on your system, restart the kubelet service and check status systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kubelet.service systemctl status kubelet -l

column21: 1. **Identify OKE Worker Nodes:** - Use the OCI Console or CLI to identify all worker nodes (compute instances) in the target OKE cluster. 2. **SSH Access to Nodes:** - SSH into each identified worker node using the appropriate credentials. 3. **Locate Kubelet Configuration File:** - Run the command: `find / -name kubelet.service` to locate the Kubelet service configuration file. The expected output should include `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 4. **Inspect the Kubelet Service Configuration:** - Open the Kubelet service config file using the command: `sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. - Look for the `--authorization-mode` argument within the file. 5. **Verify Configuration:** - Ensure that the `--authorization-mode` is set to a secure value, such as `Webhook`, and not `AlwaysAllow`. 6. **Alternative Method (if applicable):** - If you have administrative access, you can also check the Kubelet configz endpoint by running `kubectl proxy` and querying the live configuration for the status of `"authentication.*webhook":{"enabled":true}`.

column4: Ensure that the --client-ca-file argument is set as  appropriate
column5: Enable Kubelet authentication using certificates.
column6: The connections from the apiserver to the kubelet are used for fetching logs for pods, attaching (through kubectl) to running pods, and using the kubelet’s port-forwarding functionality. These connections terminate at the kubelet’s HTTPS endpoint. By default, the apiserver does not verify the kubelet’s serving certificate, which makes the connection subject to man-in-the-middle attacks, and unsafe to run over untrusted and/or public networks. Enabling Kubelet certificate authentication ensures that the apiserver could authenticate the Kubelet before submitting any requests.

column19: The control requires specific verification of the `--client-ca-file` argument in the Kubelet configuration, which necessitates direct access to the OKE nodes and inspection of configuration files. While there are suggestions for automation, the current OCI capabilities do not provide a direct method to validate this specific configuration without SSH access and manual inspection.
column21: 1. **SSH Access to OKE Nodes:** - Establish SSH access to each Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) node where the kubelet is running. Ensure you have the necessary OCI Compute instance details and follow your organization's access policy. 2. **Locate the Kubelet Service Configuration File:** - On each node, search for the kubelet service configuration file by running: ``` sudo find / -name kubelet.service ``` - The common location is `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 3. **Validate the `--client-ca-file` Argument:** - Open the kubelet service file with a text viewer: ``` sudo less /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service ``` - Inspect the command line arguments for the kubelet process. - Confirm that the `--client-ca-file` argument is present and points to the correct certificate authority file, e.g.: ``` --client-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/ca.crt ``` - The path should be a valid CA file for cluster use. 4. **(Alternative/Optional)** **Validate Live Configuration via Kubernetes API:** - Export the relevant node name from `kubectl get nodes`. - Start the Kubernetes API proxy: ``` kubectl proxy --port=8001 & ``` - Export environment variables: ``` export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 export NODE_NAME=<your-node-name> ``` - Query the kubelet configz endpoint: ``` curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" ``` - Check for the presence of `"clientCAFile":"/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt"` in the output.

column7: If using a Kubelet configuration file, check that there is an entry for --read-only- port=0. First, SSH to the relevant node: Run the following command on each node to find the appropriate Kubelet service config file: find / -name kubelet.service The output of the above command should return the file and location /etc/systemd/system/kublet.service which is the location of the Kubelet service config file. Open the Kubelet service config file: sudo more etc/systemd/system/kublet.service Verify that the --read-only-port argument exists and is set to 0. If the --read-only-port argument is not present, check that there is a Kubelet config file specified by --config. Check that if there is a readOnlyPort entry in the file, it is set to 0.

column21: 1. **SSH into each OKE node:** - Obtain the public/private SSH key and connect to each node in your Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) cluster using SSH. 2. **Locate the Kubelet service configuration file:** - Run: `find / -name kubelet.service` - Typical location: `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` 3. **Inspect the Kubelet service file:** - Open the file: `sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` - Look for the `--read-only-port` argument in the ExecStart line. - Confirm that `--read-only-port=0` is present. 4. **Check for Kubelet config file reference:** - If `--read-only-port` is not present in the service file, check for a `--config` argument instead. - Note the path to the Kubelet configuration file it points to. 5. **Inspect the Kubelet configuration file (if used):** - Open the referenced file. - Look for a `readOnlyPort` property and confirm it is set to `0`: Example: ```yaml readOnlyPort: 0 ``` 6. **If remediation is needed:** - Edit the appropriate configuration file to include or set `--read-only-port=0` or `readOnlyPort: 0`. 7. **Apply changes:** - Reload and restart the kubelet service: ``` sudo systemctl daemon-reload sudo systemctl restart kubelet.service sudo systemctl status kubelet -l ``` 8. **Repeat for all worker nodes** in the cluster.

column4: Ensure that the --streaming-connection-idle-timeout  argument is not set to 0
column5: Do not disable timeouts on streaming connections.
column6: Setting idle timeouts ensures that you are protected against Denial-of-Service attacks, inactive connections and running out of ephemeral ports. Note: By default, --streaming-connection-idle-timeout is set to 4 hours which might be too high for your environment. Setting this as appropriate would additionally ensure that such streaming connections are timed out after serving legitimate use cases.
column7: Audit Method 1: If using a Kubelet configuration file, check that there is an entry for --streaming- connection-idle-timeout is not set to 0. column4: Ensure that the --make-iptables-util-chains argument is set  to true
column5: Allow Kubelet to manage iptables.
column6: Kubelets can automatically manage the required changes to iptables based on how you choose your networking options for the pods. It is recommended to let kubelets manage the changes to iptables. This ensures that the iptables configuration remains in sync with pods networking configuration. Manually configuring iptables with dynamic pod network configuration changes might hamper the communication between pods/containers and to the outside world. You might have iptables rules too restrictive or too open.

column19: The compliance control requires direct inspection of the Kubelet configuration on the node, which involves SSH access and verification of specific parameters in the Kubelet service file. While there are potential automation opportunities, the current OCI capabilities do not provide a direct API method to retrieve or verify Kubelet configurations without manual intervention. Both AI opinions support a manual approach due to the nature of the task.
column21: 1. **SSH Access**: SSH into the relevant Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) node where the Kubernetes Kubelet is running. ```bash ssh <username>@<node_ip_address> ``` 2. **Locate Kubelet Configuration File**: Run the following command to identify the location of the Kubelet systemd service file: ```bash find / -name kubelet.service ``` 3. **Open and Verify Configuration**: Open the Kubelet configuration file, typically at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`: ```bash sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service ``` 4. **Check Argument Setting**: Look for the `--make-iptables-util-chains` parameter in the ExecStart or command line arguments. - Ensure the argument is present and set to `true` (i.e., `--make-iptables-util-chains=true`). 5. **Alternatively**: Use the Kubernetes configz API endpoint to verify the running kubelet configuration: - Set environment variables for your localhost proxy and node name based on your environment. - Start a proxy and execute a curl command: ```bash kubectl proxy --port=8001 & export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 export NODE_NAME=<node_name_from_kubectl_get_nodes> curl -sSL "http://${HOSTNAME_PORT}/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" ``` - Inspect the JSON output for `"makeIPTablesUtilChains": true`. 6. **Expected Secure Configuration**: - The Kubelet command should include: `--make-iptables-util-chains=true`

column4: Ensure that the --event-qps argument is set to 0 or a level  which ensures appropriate event capture
column5: Security relevant information should be captured. The --event-qps flag on the Kubelet can be used to limit the rate at which events are gathered. Setting this too low could result in relevant events not being logged, however the unlimited setting of 0 could result in a denial of service on the kubelet.
column6: It is important to capture all events and not restrict event creation. Events are an important source of security information and analytics that ensure that your environment is consistently monitored using the event data.

column19: The manual approach is necessary due to the requirement for direct access to the Kubelet configuration file on each node, which cannot be fully automated through OCI APIs or CLI. While there is potential for automation, the specific configuration check for the `--event-qps` parameter requires SSH access and manual verification of the service file, making a manual audit the most reliable method.
column21: 1. **Access the Node:** - Use SSH to connect to each OKE node where kubelet is running. - Command Example: `ssh opc@<oke_node_ip>` 2. **Locate the Kubelet Service Configuration File:** - Find the kubelet systemd service file, which typically contains the configuration. - Command: `find / -name kubelet.service` - Usually located at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 3. **Open the Kubelet Configuration File:** - Review the contents of the service file. - Command: `sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` 4. **Identify the --event-qps Parameter:** - Examine command-line arguments in the file. - Look for a line including `--event-qps=<VALUE>`. 5. **Verify the Parameter:** - Check that `--event-qps` is set to `0` (unlimited) or to a value appropriate for your organization’s event monitoring requirements. - **Compliant:** Parameter is present and set to `0` or another value that fulfills your policy. 6. **Alternate: Use kubelet configz Endpoint (if available):** - Start a proxy to access the kubelet API: - Run: `kubectl proxy --port=8001 &` - Get a list of node names: `kubectl get nodes` - Query the kubelet configz endpoint: - `curl -sSL "http://localhost:8001/api/v1/nodes/<NODE_NAME>/proxy/configz"` - In the output, search for `"eventRecordQPS"` and verify it is set to your organization’s standard (usually `0`). 7. **Remediation (if needed):** - If the parameter is not set correctly, edit the kubelet service config file located at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` and set the parameter `--event-qps=0`.

column19: The audit control requires specific checks on the Kubelet service configuration files that are not directly accessible via OCI APIs or CLI tools. Both AI opinions suggest a manual approach, and while there is potential for automation through SSH scripting, the lack of direct OCI API support for inspecting Kubelet configurations necessitates a manual verification process.
column21: 1. **SSH Access to Node:** - Use SSH to access each node in your Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) cluster where the Kubelet is running. - Command Example: `ssh opc@<node-ip-address>` *(Replace `<node-ip-address>` with the actual target node's IP address.)* 2. **Locate Kubelet Service Configuration:** - Find the location of the Kubelet systemd service configuration file. - Command: `find / -name kubelet.service` - The expected file is usually at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 3. **Inspect Kubelet TLS Parameters:** - Open and review the Kubelet service configuration file. - Command: `sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` - Look for the following configuration parameters: - `--tls-cert-file=/var/lib/kubelet/pki/tls.pem` - `--tls-private-key-file=/var/lib/kubelet/pki/tls.key` 4. **Validate Configuration:** - Ensure both parameters are present and reference secure PEM/key files (expected as shown above). - If these flags are missing, misconfigured, or referencing insecure/unexpected files, the node is **non-compliant**. 5. **Alternative: Using Kubelet API Configz Endpoint:** - Start a local proxy: ``` kubectl proxy --port=8001 & ``` - Obtain node name from: ``` kubectl get nodes ``` - Fetch current Kubelet config: ``` export HOSTNAME_PORT=localhost:8001 export NODE_NAME=<node-name> curl $HOSTNAME_PORT/api/v1/nodes/$NODE_NAME/proxy/configz ``` - Check the output for `tlsCertFile` and `tlsPrivateKeyFile` to ensure they are set correctly.

column4: Ensure that the --rotate-certificates argument is not set to  false
column5: Enable kubelet client certificate rotation.
column6: The --rotate-certificates setting causes the kubelet to rotate its client certificates by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there is no downtime due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself. Note: This feature also require the RotateKubeletClientCertificate feature gate to be enabled (which is the default since Kubernetes v1.7)

column19: The audit steps outlined in the compliance control require direct access to the kubelet service configuration file on each node, which necessitates SSH access and manual inspection. While there is potential for automation using OCI capabilities, the specific requirement to check the `--rotate-certificates` argument in the kubelet configuration file is not directly exposed through OCI APIs, making a manual approach necessary for compliance verification.
column21: 1. **SSH into the relevant OKE node:** - Obtain SSH access to each underlying worker node in the OKE cluster where the kubelet process runs. 2. **Locate the kubelet service configuration file:** - On each node, run: ```bash find / -name kubelet.service ``` - Typically, the file is located at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 3. **Review the kubelet service configuration:** - Open the file for reading: ```bash sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service ``` - Confirm the kubelet startup arguments. 4. **Validate `--rotate-certificates` configuration:** - Ensure that the argument `--rotate-certificates` is present AND set to `true`. - The correct line will look like: ``` ExecStart=... kubelet ... --rotate-certificates=true ... ``` - If the argument is missing or set to `false`, this is **non-compliant**. 5. **Alternative: Validate via kubelet configz API (if permitted):** - (Optional) If `kubectl` access is available, and the kubelet API is not firewalled: - Start a local API proxy: ```bash kubectl proxy --port=8001 & ``` - Export your node name (from `kubectl get nodes`) and construct the curl command: ```bash export NODE_NAME=<node-name> curl -sSL "http://localhost:8001/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" ``` - In the resulting JSON, find: ``` "rotateCertificates": true ```

column29: 1. **SSH into the relevant OKE node:** - Obtain SSH access to each underlying worker node in the OKE cluster where the kubelet process runs. 2. **Locate the kubelet service configuration file:** - On each node, run: ```bash find / -name kubelet.service ``` - Typically, the file is located at `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service`. 3. **Review the kubelet service configuration:** - Open the file for reading: ```bash sudo more /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service ``` - Confirm the kubelet startup arguments. 4. **Validate `--rotate-certificates` configuration:** - Ensure that the argument `--rotate-certificates` is present AND set to `true`. - The correct line will look like: ``` ExecStart=... kubelet ... --rotate-certificates=true ... ``` - If the argument is missing or set to `false`, this is **non-compliant**. 5. **Alternative: Validate via kubelet configz API (if permitted):** - (Optional) If `kubectl` access is available, and the kubelet API is not firewalled: - Start a local API proxy: ```bash kubectl proxy --port=8001 & ``` - Export your node name (from `kubectl get nodes`) and construct the curl command: ```bash export NODE_NAME=<node-name> curl -sSL "http://localhost:8001/api/v1/nodes/${NODE_NAME}/proxy/configz" ``` - In the resulting JSON, find: ``` "rotateCertificates": true ```

column4: Ensure that the --rotate-server-certificates argument is set  to true
column5: Enable kubelet server certificate rotation.
column6: --rotate-server-certificates causes the kubelet to both request a serving certificate after bootstrapping its client credentials and rotate the certificate as its existing credentials expire. This automated periodic rotation ensures that the there are no downtimes due to expired certificates and thus addressing availability in the CIA security triad. Note: This recommendation only applies if you let kubelets get their certificates from the API server. In case your kubelet certificates come from an outside authority/tool (e.g. Vault) then you need to take care of rotation yourself.

column6: The --rotate-certificates setting causes the kubelet to rotate its client certificates by creating new CSRs as its existing credentials expire. column21: 1. **Identify OKE Worker Nodes:** - Use the OCI Console or CLI to list all compute instances (nodes) within your OKE cluster. - Example (CLI): ```bash oci ce node-pool list --cluster-id <ocid-of-your-cluster> oci ce node-pool get --node-pool-id <ocid-of-node-pool> ``` - Note the node hostnames or IP addresses. 2. **SSH to Each Worker Node:** - SSH into each node using its public/private IP. - Ensure you use appropriate credentials/configuration as per your deployment. 3. **Locate the Kubelet Service Configuration:** - Run the following command to find the kubelet service file: ```bash sudo find / -name kubelet.service ``` - Common location: `/etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service` 4. **Inspect the Kubelet Service File:** - Open the file using a text viewer such as `less` or `cat`: ```bash sudo cat /etc/systemd/system/kubelet.service ``` - Look for the argument `--rotate-certificates`. - Verify that it is either set to `true` (`--rotate-certificates=true`) or present without being set to `false`. 5. **(Alternative) Inspect the Live Kubelet Configuration with API:** - Start a `kubectl proxy`: ```bash kubectl proxy --port=8001 & ``` - Obtain the node's name from: ```bash kubectl get nodes ``` - Run: ```bash curl -sSL "http://localhost:8001/api/v1/nodes/<NODE_NAME>/proxy/configz" ``` - Search for `rotateCertificates` to confirm its status.

column4: Ensure that the cluster-admin role is only used where  required
column5: The RBAC role cluster-admin provides wide-ranging powers over the environment and should be used only where and when needed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a set of default roles where RBAC is used. Some of these roles such as cluster-admin provide wide-ranging privileges which should only be applied where absolutely necessary. Roles such as cluster-admin allow super-user access to perform any action on any resource. When used in a ClusterRoleBinding, it gives full control over every resource in the cluster and in all namespaces. When used in a RoleBinding, it gives full control over every resource in the rolebinding's namespace, including the namespace itself.
column7: Obtain a list of the principals who have access to the cluster-admin role by reviewing the clusterrolebinding output for each role binding that has access to the cluster- admin role. kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom- columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name Review each principal listed and ensure that cluster-admin privilege is required for it.
column8: Identify all clusterrolebindings to the cluster-admin role. Check if they are used and if they need this role or if they could use a role with fewer privileges. Where possible, first bind users to a lower privileged role and then remove the clusterrolebinding to the cluster-admin role : Internal Only - General kubectl delete clusterrolebinding [name]
column10: Care should be taken before removing any clusterrolebindings from the environment to ensure they were not required for operation of the cluster. Specifically, modifications should not be made to clusterrolebindings with the system: prefix as they are required for the operation of system components.
column14: By default a single clusterrolebinding called cluster-admin is provided with the system:masters group as its principal.
column19: The audit process for ensuring that the cluster-admin role is only used where required involves a detailed review of role bindings and the necessity of each binding, which requires human judgment and consultation with cluster owners. While there is potential for automation, the complexity and need for contextual understanding make a manual approach more appropriate for this specific compliance control.
column21: 1. **Access the OKE Kubernetes cluster:** Connect to the OKE cluster using `kubectl` with sufficient privileges to view role bindings (ensure kubeconfig is configured correctly). 2. **List all ClusterRoleBindings referencing cluster-admin:** Execute the command: ``` kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o=custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECT:.subjects[*].name ``` This command will display all ClusterRoleBindings that grant access to the `cluster-admin` role, identifying which users/groups/service accounts have this binding. 3. **Review non-system ClusterRoleBindings:** Focus on ClusterRoleBindings that do not have the prefix `system:` as these may be necessary for system components. 4. **Assess business need for each principal:** For each principal (user/group/serviceaccount) with cluster-admin privileges, consult with cluster owners and administrators to determine if this privilege is essential for their role. - Confirm if the binding is still in use. - Evaluate if a role with lesser privileges could suffice. - Ensure that documentation justifies this access. 5. **Document findings:** Record all principals bound to `cluster-admin`, including justifications from step 4. Highlight any unnecessary assignments for remediation. 6. **Expected Secure Configuration:** Only system accounts and users with a clear, justified operational need should retain cluster-admin privileges.

column4: Ensure that default service accounts are not actively used.
column5: The default service account should not be used to ensure that rights granted to applications can be more easily audited and reviewed.
column6: Kubernetes provides a default service account which is used by cluster workloads where no specific service account is assigned to the pod. Where access to the Kubernetes API from a pod is required, a specific service account should be created for that pod, and rights granted to that service account. The default service account should be configured such that it does not provide a service account token and does not have any explicit rights assignments.
column7: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account. Check for automountServiceAccountToken using: export SERVICE_ACCOUNT= kubectl get serviceaccounts/$SERVICE_ACCOUNT -o yaml
column8: Create explicit service accounts wherever a Kubernetes workload requires specific access to the Kubernetes API server. Modify the configuration of each default service account to include this value automountServiceAccountToken: false Automatic remediation for the default account: kubectl patch serviceaccount default -p $'automountServiceAccountToken: false' Internal Only - General
column10: All workloads which require access to the Kubernetes API will require an explicit service account to be created.
column14: By default the default service account allows for its service account token to be mounted in pods in its namespace.
column19: While the first AI opinion suggested a manual approach, the second AI review indicated that the process could indeed be automated using scripts that leverage `kubectl` commands alongside OCI CLI or OCI Python SDK. Given that the audit steps can be executed programmatically, I have decided on an automated approach to enhance efficiency and repeatability.
column20: - OCI Resource/Service: Kubernetes Engine (OKE) - API/CLI Method: Use `kubectl` commands within a script to check service accounts - Properties to Check: - `automountServiceAccountToken` should be set to `false` - No additional roles or cluster roles bound to the default service account - Pass Criteria: - `automountServiceAccountToken: false` is present in the default service account configuration - No RoleBindings or ClusterRoleBindings associated with the default service account beyond the defaults - Fail Criteria: - `automountServiceAccountToken` is not set to `false` - Additional roles or bindings are found associated with the default service account

column4: Ensure that Service Account Tokens are only mounted  where necessary
column5: Service accounts tokens should not be mounted in pods except where the workload running in the pod explicitly needs to communicate with the API server
column6: Mounting service account tokens inside pods can provide an avenue for privilege escalation attacks where an attacker is able to compromise a single pod in the cluster. Avoiding mounting these tokens removes this attack avenue.
column7: Review pod and service account objects in the cluster and ensure that the option below is set, unless the resource explicitly requires this access. automountServiceAccountToken: false Check for automountServiceAccountToken using: export SERVICE_ACCOUNT= kubectl get serviceaccounts/$SERVICE_ACCOUNT -o yaml export POD= kubectl get pods/$POD -o yaml
column8: Modify the definition of pods and service accounts which do not need to mount service account tokens to disable it.
column10: Pods mounted without service account tokens will not be able to communicate with the API server, except where the resource is available to unauthenticated principals.
column14: By default, all pods get a service account token mounted in them.
column19: The compliance control can be effectively audited using automated methods via `kubectl`, which is the standard tool for interacting with Kubernetes resources. Both AI opinions agree on the automated approach, and the technical details provided confirm that the necessary checks can be performed programmatically without manual intervention.
column20: - OCI Resource/Service: Oracle Container Engine for Kubernetes (OKE) - API/CLI Method: `kubectl` commands - Properties to Check: - For service accounts: `automountServiceAccountToken: false` - For pods: `spec.automountServiceAccountToken: false` - Pass Criteria: Every pod and service account that does not require access to the Kubernetes API must have `automountServiceAccountToken` explicitly set to `false`. - Fail Criteria: Any pod or service account with `automountServiceAccountToken` set to `true` or left unset (default allows automount) indicates non-compliance.

column4: Ensure latest CNI version is used
column5: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
column6: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies.
column7: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports network policies. Check the DaemonSets in the kube-system namespace: Many CNI plugins operate as DaemonSets within the kube-system namespace. To see what's running: kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system Look for known CNI providers like Calico, Flannel, Cilium, etc. You can further inspect the configuration of these DaemonSets to understand more about the CNI setup: kubectl describe daemonset -n kube-system Check the CNI Configuration Files: If you have access to the nodes (via SSH), you can check the CNI configuration directly in /etc/cni/net.d/. This often requires node-level access, which might not be available depending on your permissions and the security setup of your environment.
column8: As with RBAC policies, network policies should adhere to the policy of least privileged access. Start by creating a deny all policy that restricts all inbound and outbound traffic from a namespace or create a global policy using Calico.

column14: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. Internal Only - General
column15: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
column19: The audit steps require direct interaction with the Kubernetes cluster using `kubectl`, which involves inspecting DaemonSets and potentially accessing worker nodes for configuration review. While there is some potential for automation in retrieving kubeconfig or executing commands, the specific requirement to verify the CNI plugin's support for network policies necessitates manual inspection of the DaemonSets and their configurations. This aligns with the first AI opinion, which correctly identifies the need for a manual approach.
column21: 1. **Connect to the OKE cluster using `kubectl`:** Obtain kubeconfig credentials for the target cluster using the OCI Console or CLI, then configure your local `kubectl` for that cluster. 2. **List all DaemonSets in the kube-system namespace:** Run: `kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system` Identify DaemonSets corresponding to known CNI plugins such as Calico, Flannel, Cilium, etc. 3. **Inspect the configuration and version of the CNI plugin:** For each identified CNI DaemonSet, run: `kubectl describe daemonset <daemonset-name> -n kube-system` Review the image version and configuration details to determine the CNI plugin and its version. 4. **(If necessary) Access the worker node OS for direct configuration review:** If available and permitted, SSH into a worker node and review the contents of the `/etc/cni/net.d/` directory for configuration files that specify the CNI plugin. Example: `cat /etc/cni/net.d/*` 5. **Verify support for network policies:** Consult the official documentation for the CNI version in use (e.g., the vendor’s GitHub or docs) and ensure it supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. 6. **Document findings:** Clearly record the CNI vendor, exact version, and a reference/link that confirms its support for network policies. 7. **If remediation is required:** Update the CNI plugin to a version supporting network policies, and implement a policy of least privileged access.
column27: The audit steps require direct interaction with the Kubernetes cluster using `kubectl`, which involves inspecting DaemonSets and potentially accessing worker nodes for configuration review. While there is some potential for automation in retrieving kubeconfig or executing commands, the specific requirement to verify the CNI plugin's support for network policies necessitates manual inspection of the DaemonSets and their configurations. This aligns with the first AI opinion, which correctly identifies the need for a manual approach.
column29: 1. **Connect to the OKE cluster using `kubectl`:** Obtain kubeconfig credentials for the target cluster using the OCI Console or CLI, then configure your local `kubectl` for that cluster. 2. **List all DaemonSets in the kube-system namespace:** Run: `kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system` Identify DaemonSets corresponding to known CNI plugins such as Calico, Flannel, Cilium, etc. 3. **Inspect the configuration and version of the CNI plugin:** For each identified CNI DaemonSet, run: `kubectl describe daemonset <daemonset-name> -n kube-system` Review the image version and configuration details to determine the CNI plugin and its version. 4. **(If necessary) Access the worker node OS for direct configuration review:** If available and permitted, SSH into a worker node and review the contents of the `/etc/cni/net.d/` directory for configuration files that specify the CNI plugin. Example: `cat /etc/cni/net.d/*` 5. **Verify support for network policies:** Consult the official documentation for the CNI version in use (e.g., the vendor’s GitHub or docs) and ensure it supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. 6. **Document findings:** Clearly record the CNI vendor, exact version, and a reference/link that confirms its support for network policies. 7. **If remediation is required:** Update the CNI plugin to a version supporting network policies, and implement a policy of least privileged access.

column4: Ensure that all Namespaces have Network Policies defined
column5: Use network policies to isolate traffic in your cluster network.
column6: Running different applications on the same Kubernetes cluster creates a risk of one compromised application attacking a neighboring application. Network segmentation is important to ensure that containers can communicate only with those they are supposed to. A network policy is a specification of how selections of pods are allowed to communicate with each other and other network endpoints. Network Policies are namespace scoped. When a network policy is introduced to a given namespace, all traffic not allowed by the policy is denied. However, if there are no network policies in a namespace all traffic will be allowed into and out of the pods in that namespace.
column7: Run the below command and review the NetworkPolicy objects created in the cluster. kubectl get networkpolicy --all-namespaces Ensure that each namespace defined in the cluster has at least one Network Policy. Internal Only - General
column8: Follow the documentation and create NetworkPolicy objects as you need them. Clusters you create with Container Engine for Kubernetes have flannel installed as the default CNI network provider. flannel is a simple overlay virtual network that satisfies the requirements of the Kubernetes networking model by attaching IP addresses to containers. Although flannel satisfies the requirements of the Kubernetes networking model, it does not support NetworkPolicy resources. If you want to enhance the security of clusters you create with Container Engine for Kubernetes by implementing network policies, you have to install and configure a network provider that does support NetworkPolicy resources. One such provider is Calico (refer to the Kubernetes documentation for a list of other network providers). Calico is an open source networking and network security solution for containers, virtual machines, and native host-based workloads. Use the Calico open-source software in conjunction with flannel. The Calico Enterprise does not support flannel.
column10: Once network policies are in use within a given namespace, traffic not explicitly allowed by a network policy will be denied. As such it is important to ensure that, when introducing network policies, legitimate traffic is not blocked.
column14: By default, network policies are not created.
column19: The audit approach is classified as automated because both AI opinions agree on the feasibility of using `kubectl` commands to check for NetworkPolicy objects in Kubernetes namespaces. The use of OCI CLI to authenticate and generate kubeconfig allows for seamless integration with Kubernetes, making it possible to automate the compliance check without manual intervention.

column4: Ensure clusters are created with Private Endpoint Enabled  and Public Access Disabled
column5: Disable access to the Kubernetes API from outside the node network if it is not required.
column6: In a private cluster, the master node has two endpoints, a private and public endpoint. The private endpoint is the internal IP address of the master, behind an internal load balancer in the master's VPC network. Nodes communicate with the master using the private endpoint. The public endpoint enables the Kubernetes API to be accessed from outside the master's VPC network. Although Kubernetes API requires an authorized token to perform sensitive actions, a vulnerability could potentially expose the Kubernetes publically with unrestricted access. Additionally, an attacker may be able to identify the current cluster and Kubernetes API version and determine whether it is vulnerable to an attack. Unless required, disabling public endpoint will help prevent such threats, and require the attacker to be on the master's VPC network to perform any attack on the Kubernetes API.
column7: Check for the following: export OKE_CLUSTERID= oci ce cluster get --cluster-id $OKE_CLUSTERID
column8: Default Value: By default, the Public Endpoint is disabled.
column10: This topic gives an overview of the options for enabling private access to services within Oracle Cloud Infrastructure. Private access means that traffic does not go over the internet. Access can be from hosts within your virtual cloud network (VCN) or your on- premises network. • You can enable private access to certain services within Oracle Cloud Infrastructure from your VCN or on-premises network by using either a private endpoint or a service gateway. See the sections that follow. • For each private access option, these services or resource types are available: o With a private endpoint: Autonomous Database (shared Exadata infrastructure) o With a service gateway: Available services • With either private access option, the traffic stays within the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure network and does not traverse the internet. However, if you use a service gateway, requests to the service use a public endpoint for the service. • If you do not want to access a given Oracle service through a public endpoint, Oracle recommends using a private endpoint in your VCN (assuming the service supports private endpoints). A private endpoint is represented as a private IP address within a subnet in your VCN. Internal Only - General See About Private Endpoints
column14: By default, the Public Endpoint is disabled.

column20: - OCI Resource/Service: Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) Clusters - API/CLI Method: `oci ce cluster get` - Properties to Check: - `endpoints.kubernetes.public_endpoint` (must be "None" or absent) - `endpoints.kubernetes.private_endpoint` (must be present and not "None") - Pass Criteria: - The "private endpoint" is enabled (there is a valid value for `cluster.endpoints.kubernetes.private_endpoint`) - The "public endpoint" is disabled (`cluster.endpoints.kubernetes.public_endpoint` is missing, null, or empty) - Fail Criteria: - One or more clusters have a non-empty/non-null `public_endpoint` value (indicating public access is enabled)

column4: Ensure clusters are created with Private Nodes
column5: Disable public IP addresses for cluster nodes, so that they only have private IP addresses. Private Nodes are nodes with no public IP addresses.
column6: Disabling public IP addresses on cluster nodes restricts access to only internal networks, forcing attackers to obtain local network access before attempting to compromise the underlying Kubernetes hosts.
column7: Check for the following: export OKE_NODEPOOLID= export OKE_NODEID= oci ce node-pool get --node-pool-id ${OKE_NODEPOOLID=} --node-id ${OKE_NODEID=} [--query ] [--auth ] [--profile ] [--region ]
column8: Default Value: By default, Private Nodes are disabled. Internal Only - General
column10: To enable Private Nodes, the cluster has to also be configured with a private master IP range and IP Aliasing enabled. Private Nodes do not have outbound access to the public internet. If you want to provide outbound Internet access for your private nodes, you can use Cloud NAT or you can manage your own NAT gateway.
column14: By default, Private Nodes are disabled. Internal Only - General

column20: - OCI Resource/Service: Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) Node Pools - API/CLI Method: `oci ce node-pool get` - Properties to Check: Look for the attribute `isPublicIpEnabled` or similar in the node pool configuration. - Pass Criteria: All node pools in the OKE cluster have public IPs disabled for all nodes (i.e., `isPublicIpEnabled: false`). - Fail Criteria: Any node pool allows nodes to have public IP addresses (i.e., `isPublicIpEnabled: true`).

column4: Ensure Network Policy is Enabled and set as appropriate
column5: Use Network Policy to restrict pod to pod traffic within a cluster and segregate workloads.
column6: By default, all pod to pod traffic within a cluster is allowed. Network Policy creates a pod-level firewall that can be used to restrict traffic between sources. Pod traffic is restricted by having a Network Policy that selects it (through the use of labels). Once there is any Network Policy in a namespace selecting a particular pod, that pod will reject any connections that are not allowed by any Network Policy. Other pods in the namespace that are not selected by any Network Policy will continue to accept all traffic. Network Policies are managed via the Kubernetes Network Policy API and enforced by a network plugin, simply creating the resource without a compatible network plugin to implement it will have no effect. OKE supports Network Policy enforcement through the use of Calico.
column7: Check for the following: Internal Only - General export OKE_CLUSTERID= oci ce cluster get --cluster-id $OKE_CLUSTERID Check settings for Network Policy is enabled set correctly for the cluster "kubernetesNetworkConfig": { "podsCidr": "10.244.0.0/16", "servicesCidr": "10.96.0.0/12", "networkPolicyConfig": { "isEnabled": true } }
column8: Configure Network Policy for the Cluster
column10: Network Policy requires the Network Policy add-on. This add-on is included automatically when a cluster with Network Policy is created, but for an existing cluster, needs to be added prior to enabling Network Policy. Enabling/Disabling Network Policy causes a rolling update of all cluster nodes, similar to performing a cluster upgrade. This operation is long-running and will block other operations on the cluster (including delete) until it has run to completion. If Network Policy is used, a cluster must have at least 2 nodes of type n1-standard-1 or higher. The recommended minimum size cluster to run Network Policy enforcement is 3 n1-standard-1 instances. Enabling Network Policy enforcement consumes additional resources in nodes. Specifically, it increases the memory footprint of the kube-system process by approximately 128MB, and requires approximately 300 millicores of CPU.
column14: By default, Network Policy is disabled.

column21: 1. Open the OCI Console and navigate to the "Networking" section. 2. Select "Clusters" under the "Container Engine for Kubernetes" (OKE) service. 3. Identify the specific OKE cluster you want to audit. 4. Use the OCI CLI to check the network policy settings by executing the following command: ``` oci ce cluster get --cluster-id <OKE_CLUSTER_ID> ``` 5. In the output, locate the `kubernetesNetworkConfig` section and verify that the `networkPolicyConfig` property is set to `{ "isEnabled": true }`. 6. If the network policy is not enabled, proceed to configure it by updating the cluster settings to enable network policies.

column4: Ensure access to OCI Audit service Log for OKE
column5: The audit logs are part of the OKE managed Kubernetes control plane logs managed by OKE. OKE integrates with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit Service. All operations performed by the Kubernetes API server are visible as log events on the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service.
column6: Logging is a crucial detective control for all systems to detect potential unauthorized access.
column7: Using Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Console To monitor and manage operations performed by Container Engine for Kubernetes on a particular cluster: 1. In the Console, open the navigation menu. Under Solutions and Platform, go to Developer Services and click Kubernetes Clusters. 2. Choose a Compartment you have permission to work in. 3. On the Cluster List page, click the cluster's name for which you want to monitor and manage operations. 4. The Cluster page shows information about the cluster. 5. Display the Work Requests tab, showing the recent operations performed on the cluster. To view operations performed by Container Engine for Kubernetes and the Kubernetes API server as log events in the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service: 1. In the Console, open the navigation menu. Under Governance and Administration, go to Governance and click Audit. 2. Choose a Compartment you have permission to work in. Internal Only - General 3. Search and filter to show the operations you're interested in: • To view operations performed by Container Engine for Kubernetes, enter ClustersAPI in the Keywords field and click Search. • To view operations performed by the Kubernetes API server, enter OKE API Server Admin Access in the Keywords field and click Search.
column8: No remediation is necessary for this control.
column10: The Control plane audit logs are managed by OKE. OKE Control plane logs are written to the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit Service. The Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service automatically records calls to all supported Oracle Cloud Infrastructure public application programming interface (API) endpoints as log events.
column14: By default, Kubernetes API server logs and Container Engine for Kubernetes audit events are sent to the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Audit service. By default, the Audit Log retention period is 90 days.
